page: Amazon Web Services c5a.8xlarge is a compute-optimized virtual server powered by the Nitro hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture with 32 dedicated vCPUs, derived from 16 physical cores of an AMD EPYC 7R32 processor running at 3.3 GHz. The memory configuration consists of 64.0 GB of DDR4 RAM operating at 3 GHz, yielding a resource density of 2.0 GB of memory per core. This instance does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, relying instead on network-attached storage. Network connectivity is established with a baseline bandwidth of 10 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate a distinct contrast between single-core and multi-core execution. The single-core CPU performance, measured via stress-ng, is categorized in the bottom 10%, representing a notable bottleneck for single-threaded tasks. Conversely, multi-core CPU performance and Geekbench multi-core metrics fall within the middle 50% average. Memory subsystem performance is a relative strength, with the PassMark memory score ranking in the top 25%, although large-block uncached read and write bandwidths remain average. Large-scale LLM inference is a clear limitation, with 70B model prompt processing ranking in the bottom 25%.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the c5a.8xlarge offers a dense allocation of physical AMD EPYC cores relative to its memory footprint, making it a viable option for multi-threaded workloads that do not require massive per-core memory allocations. Given its average multi-core capabilities and strong memory score, this server is suited for software compilation, multi-threaded data decompression, static web serving, and moderate database operations. It is less suitable for single-threaded applications or large-scale language model inference.
description: Amazon Web Services c5a.8xlarge is a compute-optimized instance featuring 32 dedicated vCPUs and 16 physical cores powered by an AMD EPYC 7R32 processor running at 3.3 GHz. Built on the Nitro hypervisor, it includes 64.0 GB of DDR4 memory at 3 GHz, providing 2.0 GB of RAM per core, with no local storage or GPU accelerators. Network performance is anchored by a 10 Gbps baseline bandwidth. Benchmark results reveal a significant performance tradeoff: while single-core CPU performance is poor, ranking in the bottom 10%, multi-core CPU and memory bandwidth metrics consistently achieve average ratings, and PassMark memory performance is strong. This hardware profile is qualitatively cost-efficient for parallelized compute tasks. It is best suited for multi-threaded workloads such as software compilation, static web serving, and moderate database operations, while being poorly suited for single-threaded applications or large-scale LLM inference.
og_description: Amazon Web Services c5a.8xlarge is a compute-optimized instance with 32 vCPUs, 64 GB DDR4 RAM, and a 3.3 GHz AMD EPYC processor, delivering balanced multi-core performance for parallel workloads.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services c5a.8xlarge is a compute-optimized instance featuring 32 vCPUs, 64 GB RAM, and a 3.3 GHz AMD EPYC CPU on the Nitro hypervisor.
tagline: A dedicated AMD EPYC compute-optimized virtual server featuring thirty-two vCPUs and sixty-four gigabytes of DDR4 memory for parallelized workloads.
bullet_points: Powered by a 3.3 GHz AMD EPYC 7R32 processor with 32 dedicated vCPUs
Equipped with 64.0 GB of DDR4 memory at a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio
Delivers 10 Gbps baseline network bandwidth with no local storage
Strong memory performance contrasted by weak single-core CPU execution
Suited for software compilation, static web serving, and multi-threaded tasks
